title: What is Neural Architecture Search?
concept: Neural architecture search (NAS) uses a search or optimization process to compare possible model designs inside a human-defined design space, so engineers do not have to hand-tune every architectural detail one by one.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what was EfficientNet's big scaling idea?

options.text: Scale depth, width, and image resolution in a more balanced way
Scale only width and ignore the rest forever
Remove all convolutions to save compute

exercises.explain: Exactly — EfficientNet made scaling more systematic. Now we ask a related question: what if some architecture choices are searched instead of hand-picked?

cards.title: What if you did not hand-pick every block?
cards.body: Designing a CNN often means making many choices: how many layers, which block type, how wide each stage should be, and where to downsample. Neural architecture search asks whether a computer can help explore those choices instead of a human manually trying every version one at a time.

cards.title: What NAS actually is
cards.body: Neural architecture search (NAS) is a method for searching over candidate network designs and scoring them with an objective such as validation accuracy, latency, or a combination of both. The key idea is not magic model invention — it is structured search over model options that people define ahead of time.

cards.title: Like trying menu combinations from a fixed kitchen
cards.body: Imagine a restaurant where the chef already decided the ingredients available: rice, beans, chicken, sauce, vegetables. A helper then tries many combinations to find a strong meal. NAS works similarly: humans define the ingredients and rules, then the search process explores combinations inside that menu.

cards.title: A searched CNN is still built from familiar parts
cards.body: Suppose engineers allow choices such as 3×3 vs. 5×5 convolutions, narrow vs. wide stages, optional skip connections, and different numbers of repeated blocks. NAS can train or estimate many candidate architectures from that menu and keep the ones that best match the target objective.

cards.title: NAS is not a mysterious machine with unlimited creativity
cards.body: NAS does not search every model humans can imagine, and it does not work without guidance. It searches a bounded space chosen by people, using metrics chosen by people. The search can be clever, but it is still solving the problem humans framed.

cards.title: Hand design vs. search-assisted design
cards.body: Both involve human judgment, but the workflow changes.

left.text:
Human proposes one architecture at a time
Many ideas tested manually
Iteration depends on expert intuition

right.text:
Human defines the design menu
Search compares many candidates
Good options surface faster

exercises.prompt: Which description best matches neural architecture search?

options.text: A process that searches through candidate network designs inside a human-defined space
A rule that says all good CNNs must be hand-designed from scratch
A way to skip training and guess the best architecture instantly
A tool that replaces datasets with random noise

exercises.explain: Right — NAS searches among allowed model options rather than inventing architectures out of nowhere.

exercises.prompt: True or false: NAS means humans no longer need to think about architecture design at all.
exercises.explain: False — humans still decide the search space, objective, and constraints.

exercises.sentence: NAS helps explore many candidate ___ instead of hand-testing every design choice one by one.
exercises.explain: Exactly — the core idea is structured exploration of network architectures.

exercises.prompt: Which workflow sounds most like NAS?

options.text: Define a menu of CNN blocks and let a search procedure compare many combinations
Print one architecture on paper and never evaluate any alternative
Replace the training set with a single image

exercises.explain: Yes — NAS is about exploring candidate model structures systematically.

exercises.prompt: A vision team says, 'We do not want to hand-tune every CNN detail ourselves. We want to define reasonable building-block options, then let a process compare many candidate designs.' What are they describing?

options.text: Neural architecture search, because the team is searching over a human-defined set of model choices
Data augmentation, because they are changing image colors instead of model structure
Label smoothing, because they are softening target probabilities
Feature normalization, because they are rescaling pixels before training

exercises.explain: Exactly. NAS means using search to explore architecture choices within a space people define. Next: what exactly lives inside that search space — blocks, widths, depths, and connections.
